series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] mm/filemap: place page-cache folios via an explicit mempolicy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's filemap module to explicitly specify a memory policy for page-cache allocations, rather than relying on the implicit behavior of following the first-touch task's memory policy. This change codifies the existing behavior and ensures that pagecache placement remains stable across future changes. The modification involves adding an explicit call to `folio_alloc_mpol_noprof` in the `filemap_alloc_folio_noprof` function, which takes into account the current task's memory policy for allocation decisions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] mm/mempolicy: skip task mempolicy for unmovable unaccounted kernel allocations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory policy to skip following a task's memory policy for certain types of unmovable and unaccounted kernel allocations. These allocations, such as those made by interrupt handlers or explicit placement calls, should not be influenced by a task's memory policy. The patch introduces a new helper function, alloc_task_policy(), to identify these allocation types and prevent them from following the task's policy. This change is intended to prevent performance regressions on tiered memory systems where incidental allocations can land on slower memory tiers.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series prevents a task's NUMA memory policy from influencing incidental kernel allocations, which can lead to poor placement on tiered-memory systems. The current behavior allows a single task's policy to dictate the placement of global kernel memory, even if it outlives the task or is shared by others. The fix is to only follow the task policy for movable allocations and those explicitly tied to the task via __GFP_ACCOUNT, while preferring node-local placement for unmovable and unaccounted kernel allocations. This change retains existing behavior for page cache placement and adds a filter for bare allocations in patch 2.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a bug in the memory hotplug functionality of the Linux kernel, where a failed allocation check for per-cpu node statistics (pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats) was not properly handled. The patch changes the return type of the free_area_init_core_hotplug function to int and propagates -ENOMEM up the stack when an allocation fails. This allows later online attempts to retry the allocation, ensuring that the system remains stable in case of a failed allocation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/10] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called memory_block_aligned_range() to the Linux kernel's mm/memory module. The function takes a physical address range as input and returns an aligned range, where the start address is rounded up and the end address is rounded down to memory block boundaries. This is necessary for memory hotplug operations, which require ranges to be aligned to memory blocks. The patch also updates the dax/kmem driver to use this new helper function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/10] mm/memory_hotplug: add mhp_online_type_to_str() and export string helpers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two string helpers for memory hotplug: mhp_online_type_to_str() and its inverse, mhp_online_type_from_str(). The former converts an integer online type to a human-readable string, while the latter does the reverse. Both functions are exported so that drivers can use them in their sysfs interfaces.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without raising any concerns or objections.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/10] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block` function to accept the online type through an argument, rather than calling a default value internally. This change prepares for future patches that will allow callers to specify explicit online types. The patch also updates the `add_memory_resource` function to pass the default online type via a local variable. There is no functional change in this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/10] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to access this function even when hotplug is disabled. This change enables drivers that use DAX (Direct Access) to build and function correctly in scenarios where hotplug is not enabled. The function itself simply returns `MMOP_OFFLINE`, which is a non-destructive option, ensuring compatibility with existing code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/10] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, __add_memory_driver_managed(), which allows drivers to select the preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver requires this feature to plumb its own policy through the dax kmem driver. The new function is restricted to in-tree modules using EXPORT_SYMBOL_FOR_MODULES() and does not override the system-wide onlining policy. Existing APIs are refactored to use the new helper, with no functional changes for existing users.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/10] mm/memory_hotplug: add offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() to the Linux kernel's memory_hotplug module. The function takes an array of memory ranges and processes them as one operation under a single lock, ensuring atomicity and resolving a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource. It first offlines every block in all ranges, then removes the ranges only if all are offline, reverting the whole operation if any offline fails. This provides callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step, leaving the device in a consistent state even in case of failure or interruption. The patch also updates the existing offline_and_remove_memory() function to be a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/10] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability for drivers using dax_kmem to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. It introduces a new field, 'online_type', which can be set to override the system default online policy. The system default is preserved and can still be used by setting it explicitly. This change allows for more flexibility in handling hotplug events and ensures that the ABI remains consistent even if the system default changes over time.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/10] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting initialization, cleanup, hotplug, and hot-remove logic into separate helper functions. The helpers enable future extensions to support more granular control over memory hotplug operations. The patch splits hotplug/hotunplug and init/cleanup to make resources available for hot-add, allowing re-use of the hotunplug code without destroying underlying resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/10] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to enable atomic whole-device hotplug operations for DAX kmem devices. The new 'state' attribute allows devices to be transitioned between states such as unplugged, online, and offline_kernel or offline_movable in an atomic manner. This resolves a race condition issue where another entity could operate on memory blocks while the device is being removed, causing hot-unplug failures or deadlocks. The patch also updates the unbind process to prevent resource leaks and deadlock situations.

review_comments.author: Hannes Reinecke
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch introduces two interfaces for atomic whole-device hotplug, both generating uevents, which could lead to conflicting udev rules and system instability. He suggested disabling or making configurable the generation of uevents for the old interface.
review_comments.raw_body:
Speaking of which ...
With this patch we now have _two_ interfaces to do the same thing.
And both will be generating uevents.
Which is far from ideal (one could easily envision _conflicting_
udev rules, one set doing an 'online' on the old interface,
and another set doing an 'offline' on the new interface...)
Is there a way to not sending uevents for the old interface
or to make it configurable?
The old interface had the nasty side effect of generating
_tons_ of uevents during booting, and on larger machines we
even had seen udev acting as a fork-bomb during booting,
taking down the entire machine (we had to restrict udev
to 512 threads max to avoid that from happening).
So if we could disable uevents for the old interface
things would be _so_ much easier ...

Cheers,

Hannes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/10] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to verify the functionality of dax/kmem hotplug sysfs attributes. The test checks that transitioning a kmem-backed dax device between 'unplugged', 'online' and 'online_movable' states works as expected, including idempotence and correct memory management. It also includes a destructive test to ensure force-unbinding does not deadlock. The test discovers an existing kmem-bound dax device or skips if none are available. The patch covers specific regression testing for the online -> unplug -> online_movable -> unplug sequence.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control via sysfs, allowing the state of an entire region to be controlled in a single operation. The current implementation onlines memory during probe using the system default policy, but this can lead to races between offlining and removing a whole region. The new feature provides a 'state' attribute that mirrors the per-block memoryX/state ABI, with states for unplugged, online, online_kernel, and online_movable. This allows for atomic transitions across every range of the device, solving the problem of interfering userland controllers during offline and remove steps.

review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about drivers needing to pass hotplug policy down to DAX, explaining that the mhp_get_default_online_type function needs to be exported to allow these drivers to build when hotplug is disabled. review_comments.raw_body:
Drivers which may pass hotplug policy down to DAX need MMOP_ symbols
and the mhp_get_default_online_type function for hotplug use cases.

Some drivers (cxl) co-mingle their hotplug and devdax use-cases into
the same driver code, and chose the dax_kmem path as the default driver
path - making it difficult to require hotplug as a predicate to building
the overall driver (it may break other non-hotplug use-cases).

Export mhp_get_default_online_type function to allow these drivers to
build when hotplug is disabled and still use the DAX use case.

In the built-out case we simply return MMOP_OFFLINE as it's
non-destructive.  The internal function can never return -1 either,
so we choose this to allow for defining the function with 'enum mmop'.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about drivers being unable to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL vs ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory, and responded by refactoring APIs to add a new interface that allows the dax kmem module to select a preferred policy. review_comments.raw_body:
Existing callers of add_memory_driver_managed cannot select the
preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL vs ZONE_MOVABLE), requiring it to
hot-add memory as offline blocks, and then follow up by onlining each
memory block individually.

Most drivers prefer the system default, but the CXL driver wants to
plumb a preferred policy through the dax kmem driver.

Refactor APIs to add a new interface which allows the dax kmem module
to select a preferred policy.

Overriding the configured auto-online policy is only safe for known
in-tree modules, where we know the override reflects a different,
user-requested policy.  We do not want arbitrary out-of-tree drivers
silently overriding the system-wide onlining policy, so restrict the
new interface to the kmem module using EXPORT_SYMBOL_FOR_MODULES()
rather than a plain EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL().  Other in-tree modules (e.g.
cxl_core) can be added to the allowed list as the need arises.

Refactor add_memory_driver_managed, extract __add_memory_driver_managed
- Add proper kernel-doc for add_memory_driver_managed while refactoring
- New helper accepts an explicit online_type.
- New helper validates online_type is between OFFLINE and ONLINE_MOVABLE

Refactor: add_memory_resource, extract __add_memory_resource
- new helper accepts an explicit online_type

Original APIs now explicitly pass the system-default to new helpers.

No functional change for existing users.

The author added an 'enum mmop' field to the DAX device creation path to allow drivers to specify an auto-online policy when using the kmem driver. This change introduces a new sentinel value (DAX_ONLINE_DEFAULT) that resolves the system default policy when the kmem driver binds, rather than at device-creation time.

Just attaching a fixup here, since technically it's not a bug,
could fold in separately

~Gregory

---

From: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Date: Tue, 30 Jun 2026 14:49:06 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] fixup! dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device
 hotplug

---
 drivers/dax/kmem.c | 5 ++++-
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/drivers/dax/kmem.c b/drivers/dax/kmem.c
index 19effe0da3dc..f597d8a99c1f 100644
--- a/drivers/dax/kmem.c
+++ b/drivers/dax/kmem.c
@@ -380,8 +380,11 @@ static ssize_t state_store(struct device *dev, struct device_attribute *attr,
 		return rc;
 
 	rc = dax_kmem_do_hotplug(dev_dax, data, online_type);
-	if (rc < 0)
+	if (rc < 0) {
+		/* Total failure, drop the reservations we took. */
+		dax_kmem_cleanup_resources(dev_dax, data);
 		return rc;
+	}
 
 	data->state = online_type;
 	return len;

review_comments.author: Hannes Reinecke
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Hannes Reinecke noted that the patch introduces two interfaces for the same functionality, both of which generate uevents, potentially leading to conflicting udev rules and system crashes due to excessive uevent generation during booting.
sentiment_signals: system crashes due to excessive uevent generation
review_comments.raw_body:
Speaking of which ...
With this patch we now have _two_ interfaces to do the same thing.
And both will be generating uevents.
Which is far from ideal (one could easily envision _conflicting_
udev rules, one set doing an 'online' on the old interface,
and another set doing an 'offline' on the new interface...)
Is there a way to not sending uevents for the old interface
or to make it configurable?
The old interface had the nasty side effect of generating
_tons_ of uevents during booting, and on larger machines we
even had seen udev acting as a fork-bomb during booting,
taking down the entire machine (we had to restrict udev
to 512 threads max to avoid that from happening).
So if we could disable uevents for the old interface
things would be _so_ much easier ...

Cheers,

Hannes

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem
Re: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes automatic NUMA balancing for shmem by changing alloc_pages() calls to use alloc_pages_node(), which allocates memory on the CPU-local node instead of inheriting a task's mempolicy. This prevents long-lived pages from being stranded on nodes that are not suitable for them, and reduces the risk of spreading userland data across multiple nodes. The patch targets 155 instances of alloc_pages() in various kernel modules, including net/, lib/, kexec_core/, drivers/, and arch/. It is a fix for an issue where tasks with specific mempolicies can inadvertently spread memory across multiple nodes, potentially causing performance issues or other problems.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Johannes Weiner noted that kernel allocations can be divided into two categories: those that are incidental and should not follow task policy, and those that are directly allocated on behalf of a process and should follow task policy. He suggested renaming GFP_ACCOUNT/SLAB_ACCOUNT to GFP_TASKPOLICY/SLAB_TASKPOLICY to distinguish between these cases and have mempolicy follow the new distinction.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hm. Kernel allocations that are totally incidental like the stackdepot
example above should not follow task policy. But there are kernel
allocations (kernel stack, inodes, pipes) that are directly allocated
on behalf of a process, and so probably SHOULD follow task policy.

That's an annotation problem that I think we have solved already,
because cgroups need the same distinction for what allocations to
charge to the current task's cgroup context.

We could rename __GFP_ACCOUNT / SLAB_ACCOUNT to __GFP_TASKPOLICY /
SLAB_TASKPOLICY or something, and have mempolicy follow it too.

There is still the whole "changing 20 year old behavior" aspect, but I
think the polarity works in our favor: big important allocations have
already been following the policy correctly. The behavior changes
primarily for smaller, random allocations.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that alloc_pages() calls can inherit a task mempolicy and agreed to add a trivial change to ignore task policies in certain cases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the svc_pool_for_cpu() function to route transports to a populated pool when there are fewer threads than pools, ensuring that work is serviced even if some pools have no threads.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Suggested using a different commit as the fix and proposed adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to handle the case where no pool has threads. Approved the defensive-programming approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 02 Jul 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> svc_set_num_threads() spreads the requested threads evenly across the
> service's pools (base = nrservs / sv_nrpools).  When a service runs
> fewer threads than it has pools -- e.g. an nfsd configured with fewer
> threads than the host has NUMA nodes while running in "pernode" or
> "percpu" mode -- the trailing pools are left with no threads at all.
> 
> svc_xprt_enqueue() selects a pool from the CPU servicing the transport,
> queues the transport on that pool's sp_xprts, and only wakes a thread
> from the same pool.  Each thread services exclusively its own pool, so a
> transport that lands on a threadless pool is enqueued on sp_xprts and
> never picked up: the connection hangs indefinitely.
> 
> Have svc_pool_for_cpu() skip pools that currently have no threads,
> falling back to the next populated pool.  This trades NUMA locality for
> a guarantee that the work is actually serviced.  sp_nrthreads is only
> updated under the service mutex; the lockless read here is a best-effort
> routing hint, so annotate it with data_race().
> 
> Fixes: 0f0257eaa5d2 ("svc: Move the xprt independent code to the svc_xprt.c file")

Why that commit?  Did this ever work correctly?
It seems more likely that 
Fixes: 3262c816a3d7 ("[PATCH] knfsd: split svc_serv into pools")
is appropriate.

> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  net/sunrpc/svc.c | 26 +++++++++++++++++++++++++-
>  1 file changed, 25 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> index dd80a2eaaa74..82fb7faf563f 100644
> --- a/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> +++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> @@ -402,6 +402,7 @@ struct svc_pool *svc_pool_for_cpu(struct svc_serv *serv)
>  	struct svc_pool_map *m = &svc_pool_map;
>  	int cpu = raw_smp_processor_id();
>  	unsigned int pidx = 0;
> +	unsigned int i;
>  
>  	if (serv->sv_nrpools <= 1)
>  		return serv->sv_pools;
> @@ -414,8 +415,31 @@ struct svc_pool *svc_pool_for_cpu(struct svc_serv *serv)
>  		pidx = m->to_pool[cpu_to_node(cpu)];
>  		break;
>  	}
> +	pidx %= serv->sv_nrpools;
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Threads are spread evenly across the pools, but when there are
> +	 * fewer threads than pools some pools can end up with none. A
> +	 * transport enqueued on a threadless pool would never be picked
> +	 * up, since each thread only services its own pool. Fall back to
> +	 * the next populated pool, trading NUMA locality for a guarantee
> +	 * that the transport is serviced.
> +	 */
> +	for (i = 0; i < serv->sv_nrpools; i++) {
> +		struct svc_pool *pool = &serv->sv_pools[pidx];
> +
> +		/* This is set under the sp_mutex and rarely ever changes. A
> +		 * data race here is harmless.
> +		 */
> +		if (data_race(pool->sp_nrthreads))
> +			return pool;
> +
> +		if (++pidx >= serv->sv_nrpools)
> +			pidx = 0;
> +	}
>  
> -	return &serv->sv_pools[pidx % serv->sv_nrpools];
> +	/* No pool has any threads; nothing can service the transport. */

Would a WARN_ON_ONCE() be appropriate here?

I think this is a sensible defensive-programming approach.

Reviewed-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>

Thnaks,
NeilBrown

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, removing other modes such as SVC_POOL_AUTO/GLOBAL/PERCPU/PERNODE. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts changes from a single global pool to one pool per NUMA node. This change simplifies the code and improves performance by providing proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation. The patch also removes deprecated code, including mode selection heuristics and switch statements, and updates documentation to reflect the new behavior.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that since sv_nrpools is either '1' or equal to svc_pool_map.count, it can be removed and only sv_is_pooled used for simplification and clarity in the code.

simplification which is probably best left to a later patch.
In particular, ->sv_nrpools is now either '1' or svc_pool_map.count,
and these possibilities match ->sv_is_pooled.  So we only need one of
those.
I think removing ->sv_nrpools and only using ->sv_is_pooled would
simplify/clarify some of the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per CPU-bearing node when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that each CPU-bearing node in the system has at least one thread when auto-distributing RPC services. It achieves this by guaranteeing a minimum of one thread per pool, even if it means exceeding the requested thread count.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the condition in svc_set_num_threads() and suggested an alternative solution to guarantee at least one thread per pool. Acknowledged that the original version works but might be improved.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 02 Jul 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> svc_set_num_threads() spreads the requested thread count evenly across
> the service's pools. In pernode mode each pool maps to a NUMA node, and
> svc_pool_for_cpu() steers an incoming transport to the pool for the node
> it arrived on. When fewer threads than pools are requested, even
> distribution leaves some nodes' pools empty, and a transport steered to
> an empty pool has no thread to service it.
> 
> Floor each CPU-bearing node's pool at one thread when auto-distributing a
> non-zero count, so no such pool is left empty. The resulting total may
> exceed the requested count. This only affects the auto-distribute path
> (a single-value array, i.e. svc_set_num_threads()); callers that set
> per-pool counts explicitly via svc_set_pool_threads() are unchanged and
> may still set a pool to zero. Nodes without CPUs (e.g. memory-only nodes)
> get no thread, as nothing is steered to them.
> 
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  net/sunrpc/svc.c | 15 +++++++++++++++
>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+)
> 
> diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> index c9fba7edaace..ae93a6f51087 100644
> --- a/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> +++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
> @@ -837,6 +837,12 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(svc_set_pool_threads);
>   * are multiple pools then the new threads or victims will be distributed
>   * evenly among them.
>   *
> + * When @nrservs is non-zero but smaller than the number of pools, even
> + * distribution would leave some pools empty. Since each pool maps to a
> + * NUMA node and only services transports steered to that node, every
> + * pool whose node has CPUs is instead guaranteed at least one thread.
> + * The resulting total may therefore exceed @nrservs.
> + *
>   * Caller must ensure mutual exclusion between this and server startup or
>   * shutdown.
>   *
> @@ -861,6 +867,15 @@ svc_set_num_threads(struct svc_serv *serv, unsigned int min_threads,
>  			--remain;
>  		}
>  
> +		/*
> +		 * Don't let a node's pool sit empty while threads are
> +		 * being auto-distributed: a transport steered there would
> +		 * have nothing to service it.
> +		 */
> +		if (threads == 0 && nrservs &&
> +		    nr_cpus_node(svc_pool_map_get_node(pool->sp_id)))

svc_pool_map_init_pernode() uses for_each_node_with_cpus() so we can be
certain that each node which has been allocated a pool will have at
least 1 cpu.  Thus that last condition isn't needed.

I would probably address the problem outside the loop with

if (base == 0 && nrservs != 0)
   /* We need at least one thread per pool for correct functionality */
   remain = serv->sv_nrpools;

or similar.  But your version works too and this isn't
performance-critical code.

Reviewed-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>

Thanks,
NeilBrown

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series eliminates a modulus operation in the sunrpc enqueueing codepath, replacing it with an explicit check to prevent array index out-of-bounds errors. The change is motivated by the high cost of the original modulus operation and its potential for pipeline stalls.

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/4] sunrpc: protect the svc_pool_map pool_to[] array with RCU

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch protects the svc_pool_map pool_to[] array from concurrent access by readers and writers without proper synchronization. It uses RCU (Read-Copy Update) to ensure that readers can safely access the array even when it is being updated or torn down. The patch also updates the code to use rcu_assign_pointer() and kfree_rcu_mightsleep() to properly manage the pool_to[] array's lifetime. This fix addresses a problem where svc_pool_map_get_node() could dereference a partially built or already freed pool_to[] array, leading to potential crashes.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer NeilBrown suggested an alternative solution to protect the svc_pool_map pool_to[] array by having svc_new_thread() check if the service is pooled and only call svc_pool_map_get_node() if it is, thus avoiding the need for RCU protection.

The problem only occurs when svc_create() is used as when
svc_create_pooled() is used, svc_pool_map_get() is called early so a
reference is held to svc_pool_map.

svc_new_thread() is the only caller of svc_pool_map_get_node(), and it
can easily check pool->sv_is_pooled and only call
svc_pool_map_get_node() is sv_is_pooled is true.  When false it should
probably use NUMA_NO_NODE.
e.g.

diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
index ae9ec4bf34f7..063826702f46 100644

+++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c

> > I'm going to be a bit polemic now but this seriously in good faith.
> > 
> > Why precisely do we require all this detailed information about what
> > specific coding assistant was used?
> > 
> > I find it very irritating that our git history has effectively started
> > to function a bit like a free advertising platform for a bunch of AI
> > companies and their proprietary agents and models.

FWIW, this is exactly how I feel. I added a regex to strip these in
my git hooks. So at least the net/ history should be ads-free 🤷️

Inexperienced developers who just trust the LLM output, and therefore
are the group where the tags would be most useful, tend not to add
them. Either because they are ashamed or because they want full credit.
This correlation kills the utility of the tag.

> > And it reamins unclear to me what exactly we do get out of this detailed
> > information: Do we want to run statistical analysis on what agent and
> > model is used the most and publish that on LWN at some point?
> > 
> > I acknowledge that my stance is even more radical: imho we would just
> > stop it with any disclosure requirements completely. It's useless imho.
> > We already see that other than core contributors most people don't care
> > and will just not disclose their usage of AI. I think this is entirely
> > pointless and worse it brings in undefined legal status as well. It's
> > not like recent events of pulling certain models from the face of the
> > earth have made this any less concerning.
> > 
> > But fine, if we want to do this can we please just dumb it down to
> > 
> > Assisted-by: LLM
> > 
> > or
> > 
> > Assisted-by: Coding Assistant
> > 
> > or something else. That still gives the "careful review" signal to
> > reviewers that want to pay special attention to LLM generated work while
> > avoiding this slew of metadata.
> > 
> > Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner (Amutable) <brauner@kernel.org>
> > ---
> >  Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst | 8 ++------
> >  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)
> > 
> > diff --git a/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst b/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst
> > index 899f4459c52d..fe34f3e7e828 100644
> > --- a/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst
> > +++ b/Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst
> > @@ -43,12 +43,8 @@ When AI tools contribute to kernel development, proper attribution
> >  helps track the evolving role of AI in the development process.
> >  Contributions should include an Assisted-by tag in the following format::
> >  
> > -  Assisted-by: AGENT_NAME:MODEL_VERSION [TOOL1] [TOOL2]
> > +  Assisted-by: LLM [TOOL1] [TOOL2]
> >  
> > -Where:
> > -
> > -* ``AGENT_NAME`` is the name of the AI tool or framework
> > -* ``MODEL_VERSION`` is the specific model version used
> >  * ``[TOOL1] [TOOL2]`` are optional specialized analysis tools used
> >    (e.g., coccinelle, sparse, smatch, clang-tidy)
> >  
> > @@ -56,4 +52,4 @@ Basic development tools (git, gcc, make, editors) should not be listed.
> >  
> >  Example::
> >  
> > -  Assisted-by: Claude:claude-3-opus coccinelle sparse
> > +  Assisted-by: LLM coccinelle sparse
> > 
> > ---
> > base-commit: dc59e4fea9d83f03bad6bddf3fa2e52491777482
> > change-id: 20260701-work-coding-assistants-650ae1202ee0  
> 
> 
> In general, collecting data for nebulous purposes usually turns out to
> be a bad idea. If we're not 100% clear on why we want this data, then
> we're probably better off not collecting it at all.
> 
> With that in mind: if we're going to water down the tag, then I say
> just remove the requirement altogether. If we later decide that we want
> to start collecting more detailed info for some (clear) purpose then we
> can revisit the idea.

+1

Honestly even tool attribution feels increasingly moot.
People vibe code tools and AI-in-the-loop pipelines which they never
publish. Open source tools are (hopefully?) used in pre-commit
pipelines, so they have the "kbuild bot problem" of problems getting
fixed before the code is merged. And we have the same free advertising
problem for the rest.

It's 100 times more important to drill into people to provide sufficient
information in plain English. How was the bug discovered, has it been
triggered / proven and how, what is the user impact. I wonder if
inventing tags distracts contributors from what really matters.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/18] iomap: add ->iomap_next() and iomap_process() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new callback function called ->iomap_next() that replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() functions. The ->iomap_next() function is responsible for finishing the previous mapping if needed, producing the next mapping, and allowing performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback. A helper function called iomap_process() is also added to simplify the logic required in an ->iomap_next() implementation. This change allows filesystems to be converted one at a time using the new ->iomap_next() function, which falls back to the existing ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end() path if not provided by the filesystem.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/18] xfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for XFS to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a more efficient and flexible way of handling I/O mappings. The conversion uses the iomap_process() helper function to finish previous mappings and produce the next one. This change does not introduce any functional changes and only updates the internal implementation details. The patch affects several files in the XFS codebase, including xfs_file.c and xfs_iomap.c.

The conversion involves updating the btrfs_dio_iomap_ops structure to point to the new ->iomap_next() callback.

The conversion is mechanical, with no functional changes intended. review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the iomap_next() callback being too complex and instead suggested having one ->iomap_next() callback that finishes the previous mapping if needed, and produces the next mapping. The author explained that this allows performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback, which the compiler can call directly instead of indirectly.
review_comments.raw_body:
Have one ->iomap_next() callback instead of ->iomap_begin() and
->iomap_end(). ->iomap_next() finishes the previous mapping if needed,
and produces the next mapping. This lets performance-critical callers
inline the iteration with a fixed callback, which the compiler is able
to call directly instead of indirectly.

iomap_iter() uses ->iomap_next() when the filesystem provides that
callback and otherwise falls back to the ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end()
path, so filesystems can be converted one at a time.

Add a iomap_process() inline helper that does most of the logic needed
in an ->iomap_next() implementation.

Suggested-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Suggested-by: Matthew Wilcox (Oracle) <willy@infradead.org>
Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/iomap/iter.c       | 113 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++------
 include/linux/iomap.h |  91 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++-------
 2 files changed, 171 insertions(+), 33 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/iomap/iter.c b/fs/iomap/iter.c
index e4a29829591a..1062e4e34c38 100644
--- a/fs/iomap/iter.c
+++ b/fs/iomap/iter.c
@@ -39,22 +39,7 @@ static inline void iomap_iter_done(struct iomap_iter *iter)
 		trace_iomap_iter_srcmap(iter->inode, &iter->srcmap);
 }
 
-/**
- * iomap_iter - iterate over a ranges in a file
- * @iter: iteration structue
- * @ops: iomap ops provided by the file system
- *
- * Iterate over filesystem-provided space mappings for the provided file range.
- *
- * This function handles cleanup of resources acquired for iteration when the
- * filesystem indicates there are no more space mappings, which means that this
- * function must be called in a loop that continues as long it returns a
- * positive value.  If 0 or a negative value is returned, the caller must not
- * return to the loop body.  Within a loop body, there are two ways to break out
- * of the loop body:  leave @iter.status unchanged, or set it to a negative
- * errno.
- */
-int iomap_iter(struct iomap_iter *iter, const struct iomap_ops *ops)
+static int iomap_iter_legacy(struct iomap_iter *iter, const struct iomap_ops *ops)
 {
 	bool stale = iter->iomap.flags & IOMAP_F_STALE;
 	ssize_t advanced;
@@ -114,3 +99,99 @@ int iomap_iter(struct iomap_iter *iter, const struct iomap_ops *ops)
 	iomap_iter_done(iter);
 	return 1;
 }
+
+static int iomap_iter_next(struct iomap_iter *iter, const struct iomap_ops *ops)
+{
+	int ret;
+
+	trace_iomap_iter(iter, ops, _RET_IP_);
+
+	ret = ops->iomap_next(iter, &iter->iomap, &iter->srcmap);
+	iter->status = 0;
+	if (ret > 0)
+		iomap_iter_done(iter);
+
+	return ret;
+}
+
+/**
+ * iomap_iter - iterate over a ranges in a file
+ * @iter: iteration structue
+ * @ops: iomap ops provided by the file system
+ *
+ * Iterate over filesystem-provided space mappings for the provided file range.
+ *
+ * This function handles cleanup of resources acquired for iteration when the
+ * filesystem indicates there are no more space mappings, which means that this
+ * function must be called in a loop that continues as long it returns a
+ * positive value.  If 0 or a negative value is returned, the caller must not
+ * return to the loop body.  Within a loop body, there are two ways to break out
+ * of the loop body:  leave @iter.status unchanged, or set it to a negative
+ * errno.
+ */
+int iomap_iter(struct iomap_iter *iter, const struct iomap_ops *ops)
+{
+	if (ops->iomap_next)
+		return iomap_iter_next(iter, ops);
+
+	return iomap_iter_legacy(iter, ops);
+}
+
+/**
+ * iomap_iter_continue - decide whether iteration should continue
+ * @iter: iteration structure
+ * @iomap: the mapping that was just processed
+ * @srcmap: the source mapping that was just processed
+ *
+ * Helper for ->iomap_next() implementations, normally called via
+ * iomap_process().  Called after the previous mapping has been finished to
+ * determine whether there is more of the file range left to process.
+ *
+ * Returns 1 if there is more work to do, in which case @iomap and @srcmap are
+ * cleared so the caller can produce the next mapping; zero if the range is
+ * fully consumed; or a negative errno on error.  Any folio batch attached to
+ * the mapping is released before returning.
+ */
+int iomap_iter_continue(const struct iomap_iter *iter, struct iomap *iomap,
+		struct iomap *srcmap, int ret)
+{
+	bool stale = iomap->flags & IOMAP_F_STALE;
+	ssize_t advanced = iter->pos - iter->iter_start_pos;
+
+	if (!iomap->length)
+		return 1;
+
+	/*
+	 * Use iter->len to determine whether to continue onto the next mapping.
+	 * Explicitly terminate on error status or if the current iter has not
+	 * advanced at all (i.e. no work was done for some reason) unless the
+	 * mapping has been marked stale and needs to be reprocessed.
+	 */
+	if (ret < 0 && !advanced)
+		return ret;
+
+	/* detect old return semantics where this would advance */
+	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(iter->status > 0))
+		ret = -EIO;
+	else if (iter->status < 0)
+		ret = iter->status;
+	else if (iter->len == 0 || (!advanced && !stale))
+		ret = 0;
+	else
+		ret = 1;
+
+	if (iomap->flags & IOMAP_F_FOLIO_BATCH) {
+		folio_batch_release(iter->fbatch);
+		folio_batch_reinit(iter->fbatch);
+		iomap->flags &= ~IOMAP_F_FOLIO_BATCH;
+	}
+
+	if (ret <= 0)
+		return ret;
+
+	memset(iomap, 0, sizeof(*iomap));
+	memset(srcmap, 0, sizeof(*srcmap));
+
+	return ret;
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(iomap_iter_continue);
diff --git a/include/linux/iomap.h b/include/linux/iomap.h
index 3582ed1fe236..8a78f47c557b 100644
--- a/include/linux/iomap.h
+++ b/include/linux/iomap.h
@@ -212,24 +212,35 @@ struct iomap_write_ops {
 #define IOMAP_ATOMIC		(1 << 9) /* torn-write protection */
 #define IOMAP_DONTCACHE		(1 << 10)
 
-struct iomap_ops {
-	/*
-	 * Return the existing mapping at pos, or reserve space starting at
-	 * pos for up to length, as long as we can do it as a single mapping.
-	 * The actual length is returned in iomap->length.
-	 */
-	int (*iomap_begin)(struct inode *inode, loff_t pos, loff_t length,
-			unsigned flags, struct iomap *iomap,
-			struct iomap *srcmap);
+/*
+ * Return the existing mapping at pos, or reserve space starting at pos for up
+ * to length, as long as we can do it as a single mapping.
+ * The actual length is returned in iomap->length.
+ */
+typedef int (*iomap_begin_fn)(struct inode *inode, loff_t pos, loff_t length,
+		unsigned flags, struct iomap *iomap, struct iomap *srcmap);
 
-	/*
-	 * Commit and/or unreserve space previous allocated using iomap_begin.
-	 * Written indicates the length of the successful write operation which
-	 * needs to be commited, while the rest needs to be unreserved.
-	 * Written might be zero if no data was written.
-	 */
-	int (*iomap_end)(struct inode *inode, loff_t pos, loff_t length,
-			ssize_t written, unsigned flags, struct iomap *iomap);
+/*
+ * Commit and/or unreserve space previous allocated using iomap_begin.
+ * Written indicates the length of the successful write operation which needs
+ * to be commited, while the rest needs to be unreserved.
+ * Written might be zero if no data was written.
+ */
+typedef int (*iomap_end_fn)(struct inode *inode, loff_t pos, loff_t length,
+		ssize_t written, unsigned flags, struct iomap *iomap);
+
+/*
+ * Produce the next mapping (finishing the previous one if needed).
+ * Return 1 to continue iterating, 0 if the range is fully consumed, or a
+ * negative error on failure.
+ */
+typedef int (*iomap_next_fn)(const struct iomap_iter *iter, struct iomap *iomap,
+		struct iomap *srcmap);
+
+struct iomap_ops {
+	iomap_begin_fn iomap_begin;
+	iomap_end_fn iomap_end;
+	iomap_next_fn iomap_next;
 };
 
 /**
@@ -317,6 +328,9 @@ static inline const struct iomap *iomap_iter_srcmap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
 	return &i->iomap;
 }
 
+int iomap_iter_continue(const struct iomap_iter *iter, struct iomap *iomap,
+		struct iomap *srcmap, int ret);
+
 /*
  * Return the file offset for the first unchanged block after a short write.
  *
@@ -648,4 +662,47 @@ static inline void iomap_bio_readahead(struct readahead_control *rac,
 }
 #endif /* CONFIG_BLOCK */
 
+/**
+ * iomap_process - finish the previous mapping and produce the next one
+ * @iter: iteration structure
+ * @iomap: mapping to finish and then repopulate
+ * @srcmap: source mapping to finish and then repopulate
+ * @begin: callback that produces a mapping for the current position
+ * @end: optional callback that finishes the previous mapping, or NULL
+ *
+ * Inline helper that implements the common body of an ->iomap_next()
+ * callback: it finishes the previous mapping via @end (if present), decides
+ * via iomap_iter_continue() whether to keep going, and obtains the next
+ * mapping via @begin.
+ *
+ * Returns 1 to continue iterating, 0 once the range is fully consumed, or a
+ * negative errno on error.
+ */
+static __always_inline int iomap_process(const struct iomap_iter *iter,
+		struct iomap *iomap, struct iomap *srcmap, iomap_begin_fn begin,
+		iomap_end_fn end)
+{
+	int ret = 0;
+
+	if (iomap->length && end) {
+		ssize_t advanced = iter->pos - iter->iter_start_pos;
+		loff_t len;
+
+		len = iomap_length_trim(iter, iter->iter_start_pos,
+				iter->len + advanced);
+
+		ret = end(iter->inode, iter->iter_start_pos, len, advanced,
+				iter->flags, iomap);
+	}
+
+	ret = iomap_iter_continue(iter, iomap, srcmap, ret);
+	if (ret <= 0)
+		return ret;
+
+	ret = begin(iter->inode, iter->pos, iter->len, iter->flags, iomap,
+			srcmap);
+
+	return ret < 0 ? ret : 1;
+}
+
 #endif /* LINUX_IOMAP_H */
--

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/6] fuse: decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the creation of fuse_ring from entry registration in FUSE connections. The ring is now created during initialization after a server has negotiated and set FUSE_OVER_IO_URING, rather than being lazily created on the first FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER command. This change eliminates concurrent creation races and simplifies the code by removing open-coded handling in fuse_uring_create().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/6] fuse: add FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE command to the Linux kernel's fuse module, allowing servers to create queues explicitly and decoupling queue setup from entry registration. This change is necessary for the introduction of buffer pools, which require existing queues to be created first. The patch modifies the fuse_ring_queue creation logic to handle explicit queue creation and adds a new function fuse_uring_add_queue to handle this command.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous chunk of memory to the kernel for servicing requests. This decouples the buffer allocation from individual entries and enables the kernel to optimize memory usage by reusing buffers across multiple requests. The patch introduces a new command, FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL, for registering a buffer pool per queue, and modifies the io-uring implementation to use a bitmap to track free buffers and report their byte offset to the server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/6] fuse: support registered buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use a shared buffer pool that is also registered through io-uring. This eliminates the overhead of pinning and unpining user pages per I/O request, improving performance on write-intensive workloads. To use this feature, servers must set the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFPOOL flag when adding a bufpool and include the index of the registered buffer pool in every submitted sqe. The patch includes benchmark results showing improved throughput for direct writes using registered buffers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/6] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. This is achieved by registering relevant pages into a sparse slot in the server's io_uring registered buffer table and using fixed buffer operations. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN capability and bufpools, and introduces a new flag FOPEN_IO_URING_ZERO_COPY for servers to specify which open files should use zero-copy. The request flow is modified to skip data copying when possible, reducing memory overhead and improving performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/6] docs: fuse: document io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy uapi
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for using the FUSE file system over io-uring, specifically covering buffer pools and zero-copy functionality. It introduces new commands and flags to enable these features, including adding a queue, registering a buffer pool, and enabling zero-copy. The documentation is split across three files: fuse-io-uring.rst, index.rst, and uapi/io-uring.rst. This patch aims to provide clear guidance for users on how to utilize the FUSE file system with io-uring's advanced features.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy to the FUSE file system. The current implementation tightly couples entries and buffers, requiring a dedicated payload buffer for each entry. This patch decouples them by allowing servers to pass in a buffer pool that the kernel can use as needed. Additionally, it introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows servers to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The benefits of this patch include reduced memory usage requirements and improved throughput, particularly when backing I/O is not the bottleneck.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: mempolicy: fix automatic numa balancing for shmem

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where mapped shared memory (shmem) was not considered for NUMA balancing on tiered systems. The issue arose from a previous commit that intended to limit NUMA scanning to migrate-on-fault VMAs, but incorrectly treated the absence of a shared policy as an explicit opt-out. The patch changes the vma_policy_mof function to use __get_vma_policy directly, allowing default policy VMAs to participate in balancing while still excluding explicitly pinned memory. This fix should improve performance on tiered systems with cachelib-based workloads.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gregory Price expressed strong disagreement (CONTENTIOUS) with the patch, stating that demotions don't care about mempolicy and that opting shmem out of NUMA balancing on a tiered system is problematic. He also emphasized that this issue highlights the need for a complete overhaul of the demotion mechanism to prevent undefined behavior in memory placement.

Gave Reviewed-by
review_comments.raw_body:
Eugh.

Demotions don't care about mempolicy, so opting shmem out of NUMA

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gregory Price noted that task-wide policies can be problematic, citing an example where a stack depot pool inherits the task's NUMA memory policy and becomes stranded on a node forever. He also highlighted the issue of alloc_pages() calls inheriting task mempolicies in various parts of the kernel, which can lead to unexpected behavior such as spreading userland data across nodes.

Reviewer noted that direct reclaim and kswapd have different cgroup considerations, making cross-cpuset checks potentially complex to implement.
review_comments.raw_body:
It can be, but there's also many footguns with task-wide policy.

Something i found while seeing if i could make ZONE_NORMAL nodes more
reliably hotpluggable:

diff --git a/lib/stackdepot.c b/lib/stackdepot.c
index dd2717ff94bf..9ceeb56574ef 100644
--- a/lib/stackdepot.c
+++ b/lib/stackdepot.c
@@ -682,7 +682,15 @@ depot_stack_handle_t stack_depot_save_flags(unsigned long *entries,
 	 * we won't be able to do that under the lock.
 	 */
 	if (unlikely(can_alloc && !READ_ONCE(new_pool))) {
-		page = alloc_pages(gfp_nested_mask(alloc_flags),
+		/*
+		 * The stack depot pool is a global, never-freed allocation.
+		 * Use alloc_pages_node() on the CPU-local node instead of
+		 * alloc_pages() so the pool does not inherit a transient task's
+		 * NUMA mempolicy (e.g. MPOL_BIND to a CPU-less/bound node), which
+		 * would strand this long-lived page on that node forever.
+		 */
+		page = alloc_pages_node(numa_node_id(),
+				   gfp_nested_mask(alloc_flags),
 				   DEPOT_POOL_ORDER);
 		if (page)
 			prealloc = page_address(page);

This is a global, permanently allocated, resource that inherits a task
mempolicy's placement because that task *happened* to be the first one
to touch it.

There are many alloc_pages() calls (155 instances kernel-wide) that
inherit a task mempolicy when that's probably not what we want.

alloc_pages() is called in: net/, lib/, kexec_core/, drivers/, arch/

you can imagine a task setting `set_mempolicy(INTERLEAVE, ALL)` and the
result is a bunch of random driver memory gets spread all over the place
along with the task's heap.  Is that really what the caller wanted, or
did they just want userland data spread about?

But at this point it's a 20 year old interface, not much we can do about
it without making *someone* sad :[

I considered proposing MPOL_F_MOVABLE_ONLY to mean (roughly) "userland
memory only" - and then slowly trying to migrate numactl to make this
the default.

---

Direct reclaim considers cpusets for the reclaiming task (added
recently), kswapd sits in its own cgroup.

Cross-cpuset checks - i'm not sure how tractable that is.  We ignore it
for now, recognizing that if something is cross-cpuset it's some
definition of shared/global object (e.g. pagecache mappings).

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Johannes Weiner noted that kernel allocations can be divided into two categories: those that are incidental and should not follow task policy, and those that are directly allocated on behalf of a process and should follow task policy. He suggested renaming GFP_ACCOUNT/SLAB_ACCOUNT to GFP_TASKPOLICY/SLAB_TASKPOLICY to distinguish between these cases and have mempolicy follow the new distinction.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hm. Kernel allocations that are totally incidental like the stackdepot
example above should not follow task policy. But there are kernel
allocations (kernel stack, inodes, pipes) that are directly allocated
on behalf of a process, and so probably SHOULD follow task policy.

That's an annotation problem that I think we have solved already,
because cgroups need the same distinction for what allocations to
charge to the current task's cgroup context.

We could rename __GFP_ACCOUNT / SLAB_ACCOUNT to __GFP_TASKPOLICY /
SLAB_TASKPOLICY or something, and have mempolicy follow it too.

There is still the whole "changing 20 year old behavior" aspect, but I
think the polarity works in our favor: big important allocations have
already been following the policy correctly. The behavior changes
primarily for smaller, random allocations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: compaction: support non-movable compaction for pageblock requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's compaction code to support non-movable direct compaction for pageblock requests. It allows whole-block requests, which are essentially type neutral, to scan blocks of other types and fixes the protection gates to take the requested order into account. The only exception is CMA (Contiguous Memory Allocation) blocks, which remain strictly typed due to their sticky nature. This change aims to improve the efficiency of non-movable direct compaction, particularly in defrag_mode where whole-block production is a goal.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author questioned the motivation behind restricting async compaction to pageblocks of same migratetype, suggesting that this behavior may not be desired and could be improved by applying the block pollution guard only when allocation fallbacks are likely.
review_comments.raw_body:
Let's see. Originally it came from here:

commit 9927af740b1b9b1e769310bd0b91425e8047b803
Author: Mel Gorman <mel@csn.ul.ie>
Date:   Thu Jan 13 15:45:59 2011 -0800

    mm: compaction: perform a faster migration scan when migrating asynchronously

This limited async scanners to movable blocks. By keeping them to the
most productive space, it keeps their latencies down.

But then there was a follow up here:

commit 282722b0d258ec23fc79d80165418fee83f01736
Author: Vlastimil Babka <vbabka@kernel.org>
Date:   Mon May 8 15:54:49 2017 -0700

    mm, compaction: restrict async compaction to pageblocks of same migratetype

This made the migratetype filtering about preventing block
pollution. The patch quotes reduced extfrag numbers.

So now we have a block pollution guard that we apply only if... the
scanner is latency sensitive? :) Is this actually desired behavior?

Another way of looking at it would be this:

	/*
	 * Allocation fallbacks can spread migratable pages
	 * into non-movable blocks. This is high-effort,
	 * low-result work. Restrict it to sync scans.
	 */

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not prevent non-movable pages from being moved into movable blocks outside of defrag mode, and requested consideration for this scenario.
review_comments.raw_body:
But also vice versa, non-movable pages into movable blocks? (without
defrag_mode?).

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing Vlastimil's concern about non-movable compaction requests being allowed to scan movable blocks, even though they are not compactable anymore. The author acknowledges this issue and explains that the behavior for movable and non-movable requests is asymmetrical, with movable requests first trying high-effort mode on unmovable blocks before escalating, whereas non-movable requests start in high-effort mode but can escalate when it doesn't produce results.
review_comments.raw_body:
Uhm but those aren't compactable anymore then, right?

There is a flipside, but it isn't quite symmetrical. Movable requests
are allowed to look for movables in unmovable blocks once they become
sync (high-effort, low-result). Non-movable requests are finally
allowed to empty movable blocks once they become sync; they *start*
with the high-effort, low-result mode to avoid block contamination but
are allowed to escalate when that doesn't produce results.

I'll try to work that second part into the comment as well.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Zi Yan suggested clarifying the definition of 'small' in the patch description, specifically that it refers to pageblock_order requests
review_comments.raw_body:

better to spell it out to prevent confusion.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: page_alloc: move capture_control to the page allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the per-task capture_control to the page allocator, allowing it to carry allocation-side information that compaction's compact_control does not. The capture_control is passed through try_to_compact_pages() and compact_zone_order() instead of a bare struct page **. This change is necessary because defrag_mode promotes sub-block allocations to pageblock-order compaction, which targets the larger order, while capture should remain at the original allocation order. No functional changes are made by this patch.

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested an alternative approach for handling whole-pageblock pages, but noted that the current patch also works

Reviewer Vlastimil suggested simplifying the code by removing the capc->cc struct and accessing only necessary fields directly in try_to_compact_pages(), which would eliminate the need for compact_zone_order() to set up capc fields.

Reviewer Vlastimil (SUSE) questioned the necessity of explicit initialization of all fields in the capture_control struct, suggesting that either implicit initialization with a barrier is sufficient or every field should be explicitly set to NULL/zero.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well I guess you could also try to capture the whole-pageblock page and then
deal with it as with whole pageblock stealing? But this works too and
perhaps it's simpler.

---

I wonder if it makes sense to continue having capc->cc and this whole dance
in two functions. AFAICS (after patch 4/4) we access only capc->cc->zone and
capc->cc->migratetype. migratetype is stable in the whole
try_to_compact_pages(), could be part of capc. Order can be added by this
patch (with no semantic change to it) and not the next one. Zone varies, but
could be also in capc and set by try_to_compact_pages() before every call to
compact_zone_order(). Then compact_zone_order() doesn't have to set up any
capc fields anymore?

---

So either an implicit { } NULL / zero initialization + barrier() is enough
(I hope so) and we don't need to set NULL / zero in every field explicitly.
Or not and then we should set every field and not just page.

Author acknowledged that initializing capture_control partially would zero out its other fields, but didn't see it as a correctness issue; no fix planned.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: page_alloc: fix non-movable reclaim storm in defrag_mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where non-movable page allocations cause excessive reclaim storms in defrag_mode, leading to performance issues and OOM triggers. The issue arises when non-movable requests retry on reclaim, causing them to spin and over-reclaim excessively. To fix this, the patch sends non-movable requests directly into pageblock reclaim/compaction, allowing them to help move pages along and meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar. This is a temporary solution until a longer-term plan can be implemented to have all requests, including movable ones, contribute to block creation and reduce allocation latencies.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a performance issue in the Linux kernel's memory management system, specifically when vm.defrag_mode is enabled. The problem arises from non-movable requests spinning and reclaiming unproductively due to kswapd/kcompactd being overwhelmed. To fix this, the patches modify the allocator slowpath to allow non-movable requests to participate in pageblock production, which has been successfully tested on an affected workload for two weeks.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Zi Yan pointed out that the comment 'small unmovable/reclaimable requests' in compaction.c is ambiguous and could be clarified by explicitly stating that it refers to requests smaller than pageblock_order.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri Jun 26, 2026 at 2:21 PM EDT, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> As we deployed defrag_mode into Meta production, pressure spikes and
> excessive swapping were observed on some workloads. Tracing confirmed
> that this is unmovable/reclaimable requests spinning in the allocator
> and direct reclaim, causing excessive amounts of swap.
>
> The initial plan for defrag_mode was to rely on kswapd/kcompactd to
> produce blocks, and if those are overwhelmed under high pressure, let
> the allocator fall back (__rmqueue_steal()) after its retry loops.
> However, that retrying results in more reclaim on some of these
> workloads than we'd hoped, sometimes excessively so, spurred on by the
> !costly order conditions in should_reclaim_retry().
>
> The storms are dependent on the request type. Reclaim will inevitably
> make room in existing movable blocks, since that's where the LRU pages
> live. So if movable requests retry on reclaim, they make progress.
>
> When non-movable requests spin in reclaim that isn't productive. They
> cannot use the individually freed pages, and the process is unlikely
> to accidentally free whole blocks to meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar.
> They spin and overreclaim excessively, which tanks performance and
> triggers userspace guards like swap exhaustion or pressure based OOM.
>
> To fix this, send non-movable requests, regardless of order, into
> pageblock reclaim/compaction. This way, they help move things along to
> meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar. After this patch, the reclaim storms
> and excess OOM rates are no longer observed in production.
>
> The longer-term plan is still to have all requests, including the
> movable ones, help make blocks to spread the cost of defragmenting
> more evenly and fairly; combined with proper watermarking to reduce
> allocation latencies in the common case. However, doing this naively
> unearths scaling and concurrency limitations in compaction that need
> to be addressed first. Promoting just non-movables for now is the
> minimally viable bug fix for the above issue.
>
> Fixes: f38356df6474 ("mm: page_alloc: introduce defrag_mode")

Should be
Fixes: e3aa7df331bc ("mm: page_alloc: defrag_mode").
Since I cannot find f38356df6474 in the tree.

> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

-- 
Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

---

On Fri Jun 26, 2026 at 2:21 PM EDT, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> While trying to fix a reclaim storm in defrag_mode, I noticed that
> non-movable direct compaction is extremely inefficient.
>
> When searching for space to evacuate, compaction only allows blocks of
> the same type as the incoming request. This is to prevent migratetype
> pollution, where a small non-movable request frees space in a movable
> block and provokes the allocator to fall back and pollute it.
>

> makes non-movable direct compaction nearly useless: if we get the type
> annotations right, by definition there aren't any movable pages inside
> the non-movable blocks it is allowed to scan.
>
> With defrag_mode, the goal is the production of whole blocks, which
> are essentially type neutral: __rmqueue_claim() will convert them

>
> Fix the pollution gates to take the requested order into account, and
> allow whole-block requests to scan blocks of other types.
>
> The only exception is CMA blocks. That type is sticky and these blocks
> cannot be claimed to other types. Continue to be strict with them, and

> -	 * reduces the risk that a large movable pageblock is freed for
> -	 * an unmovable/reclaimable small allocation.
> +	 * Prevent small unmovable/reclaimable requests from polluting

This "small" as well.

> +	 * movable blocks through fallbacks. Whole-block production is
> +	 * exempt as the allocator claims and converts these.
>  	 */

    mm: compaction: perform a faster migration scan when migrating asynchronously

This limited async scanners to movable blocks. By keeping them to the
most productive space, it keeps their latencies down.

But then there was a follow up here:

This made the migratetype filtering about preventing block
pollution. The patch quotes reduced extfrag numbers.

So now we have a block pollution guard that we apply only if... the
scanner is latency sensitive? :) Is this actually desired behavior?

Another way of looking at it would be this:

	/*

	 * into non-movable blocks. This is high-effort,
	 * low-result work. Restrict it to sync scans.

On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 08:06:03PM +0200, Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) wrote:
> On 6/26/26 20:21, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > As we deployed defrag_mode into Meta production, pressure spikes and
> > excessive swapping were observed on some workloads. Tracing confirmed
> > that this is unmovable/reclaimable requests spinning in the allocator
> > and direct reclaim, causing excessive amounts of swap.
> > 
> > The initial plan for defrag_mode was to rely on kswapd/kcompactd to
> > produce blocks, and if those are overwhelmed under high pressure, let
> > the allocator fall back (__rmqueue_steal()) after its retry loops.
> > However, that retrying results in more reclaim on some of these
> > workloads than we'd hoped, sometimes excessively so, spurred on by the
> > !costly order conditions in should_reclaim_retry().
> > 
> > The storms are dependent on the request type. Reclaim will inevitably
> > make room in existing movable blocks, since that's where the LRU pages
> > live. So if movable requests retry on reclaim, they make progress.
> > 
> > When non-movable requests spin in reclaim that isn't productive. They
> > cannot use the individually freed pages, and the process is unlikely
> > to accidentally free whole blocks to meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar.
> > They spin and overreclaim excessively, which tanks performance and
> > triggers userspace guards like swap exhaustion or pressure based OOM.
> > 
> > To fix this, send non-movable requests, regardless of order, into
> > pageblock reclaim/compaction. This way, they help move things along to
> > meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar. After this patch, the reclaim storms
> > and excess OOM rates are no longer observed in production.
> > 
> > The longer-term plan is still to have all requests, including the
> > movable ones, help make blocks to spread the cost of defragmenting
> > more evenly and fairly; combined with proper watermarking to reduce
> > allocation latencies in the common case. However, doing this naively
> > unearths scaling and concurrency limitations in compaction that need
> > to be addressed first. Promoting just non-movables for now is the
> > minimally viable bug fix for the above issue.
> > 
> > Fixes: f38356df6474 ("mm: page_alloc: introduce defrag_mode")
> 
> That's from 6.15. Do you intend any stable backporting, or we just mark it
> as a heads up for anyone who tracks fixes and might consider it.

Good point, let's Cc: stable.

I doubt there are many defrag_mode users at this point, but this is
quite the handgrenade that went off in production once already and was
a pain to debug.

There is a flipside, but it isn't quite symmetrical. Movable requests
are allowed to look for movables in unmovable blocks once they become
sync (high-effort, low-result). Non-movable requests are finally
allowed to empty movable blocks once they become sync; they *start*
with the high-effort, low-result mode to avoid block contamination but
are allowed to escalate when that doesn't produce results.

I'll try to work that second part into the comment as well.

On 6/26/26 20:21, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> As we deployed defrag_mode into Meta production, pressure spikes and
> excessive swapping were observed on some workloads. Tracing confirmed
> that this is unmovable/reclaimable requests spinning in the allocator
> and direct reclaim, causing excessive amounts of swap.
> 
> The initial plan for defrag_mode was to rely on kswapd/kcompactd to
> produce blocks, and if those are overwhelmed under high pressure, let
> the allocator fall back (__rmqueue_steal()) after its retry loops.
> However, that retrying results in more reclaim on some of these
> workloads than we'd hoped, sometimes excessively so, spurred on by the
> !costly order conditions in should_reclaim_retry().
> 
> The storms are dependent on the request type. Reclaim will inevitably
> make room in existing movable blocks, since that's where the LRU pages
> live. So if movable requests retry on reclaim, they make progress.
> 
> When non-movable requests spin in reclaim that isn't productive. They
> cannot use the individually freed pages, and the process is unlikely
> to accidentally free whole blocks to meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar.
> They spin and overreclaim excessively, which tanks performance and
> triggers userspace guards like swap exhaustion or pressure based OOM.
> 
> To fix this, send non-movable requests, regardless of order, into
> pageblock reclaim/compaction. This way, they help move things along to
> meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar. After this patch, the reclaim storms
> and excess OOM rates are no longer observed in production.
> 
> The longer-term plan is still to have all requests, including the
> movable ones, help make blocks to spread the cost of defragmenting
> more evenly and fairly; combined with proper watermarking to reduce
> allocation latencies in the common case. However, doing this naively
> unearths scaling and concurrency limitations in compaction that need
> to be addressed first. Promoting just non-movables for now is the
> minimally viable bug fix for the above issue.
> 
> Fixes: f38356df6474 ("mm: page_alloc: introduce defrag_mode")

That's from 6.15. Do you intend any stable backporting, or we just mark it
as a heads up for anyone who tracks fixes and might consider it.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/4] mm: compaction: support non-movable compaction for pageblock requests
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch extends the support for compaction of pageblock requests to include non-movable blocks, which was previously limited to movable blocks. This change is a follow-up to earlier patches that restricted asynchronous compaction to prevent block pollution and improve performance. The patch aims to allow allocation fallbacks to compact non-movable pages, reducing the effort required for compaction while still achieving the desired results.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not consider the case of non-movable pages being allocated in movable blocks, and suggested considering this scenario as well.
sentiment_signals: requested consideration for additional edge case
review_comments.raw_body:
But also vice versa, non-movable pages into movable blocks? (without
defrag_mode?).

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a concern about non-movable compaction requests and explains the current behavior, stating that movable requests are allowed to look for movables in unmovable blocks once they become sync.
review_comments.raw_body:
Uhm but those aren't compactable anymore then, right?

There is a flipside, but it isn't quite symmetrical. Movable requests
are allowed to look for movables in unmovable blocks once they become
sync (high-effort, low-result). Non-movable requests are finally
allowed to empty movable blocks once they become sync; they *start*
with the high-effort, low-result mode to avoid block contamination but
are allowed to escalate when that doesn't produce results.

I'll try to work that second part into the comment as well.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] iommu/arm-smmu-v3: Shrink command/event/PRI queues in kdump kernel

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch shrinks the command, event, and PRI queues in the kdump kernel to a single page size, reducing memory usage on systems with multiple SMMUv3 instances. The change is made by adding a conditional check for the kdump kernel mode in arm_smmu_init_one_queue(), which clamps each queue's maximum size based on the entry size and page shift.

review_comments.author: Jason Gunthorpe
review_comments.summary: Jason expressed agreement with the patch's approach and asked a question about the use of min_t(). He inquired why it was needed in this specific context.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 04:45:28PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) wrote:
> The command, event and PRI queues are sized from the maxima the hardware
> advertises in IDR1, which can be several megabytes each. On systems with
> many SMMUv3 instances that cost is paid per instance and adds up to tens
> of megabytes of coherent DMA in the capture kernel.
> 
> A kdump capture kernel runs from a small crashkernel reservation and only
> has to drive the few devices used to save the dump, so deep queues serve
> no purpose. The queues carry invalidation commands and fault records, not
> DMA data, so dump throughput is unaffected; a shallower queue only bounds
> how many commands may be in flight before a sync, which does not matter for

> in one place. The command queue still holds at least one batch plus a sync
> (256 entries on a 4K-page kernel, well above CMDQ_BATCH_ENTRIES), so
